content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-5441

May 25, 1976

Mr. William J. Caven
Secretary, Board of Trustees
Biggs Unified School District
P.O. Box 397
Biggs, California 95917

Dear Mr. Caven:

The resolution of the Biggs Unified School District Board of Trustees calling for legislation exempting school board members from the Political Reform Act's disclosure provisions has been forwarded to the Fair Political Practices Commission by Governor Brown's office.

Section 81012(a) of the Political Reform Act states in part that the Act "may be amended to further its purposes by statute, passed in each house by roll call vote entered in the journal, two-thirds of the membership concurring and signed by the Governor...." The Act also "may be amended or repealed by a statute that becomes effective only when approved by the electors." (Section 81012(b))

The question that might be asked is, "Does the exemption legislation sought by the Biggs Board of Trustees further the purposes of the Political Reform Act?" The answer is that it does not.

When approximately 70 per cent of the voters cast their ballots in favor of the Political Reform Act in 1974, they clearly were endorsing the following concepts which are stated in the Act:

"State and local government should serve the needs and respond to the wishes of all citizens equally, without regard to their wealth." (Section 81001(a))

"Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b))

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
(Section 87100)

In an effort to realize these purposes, so strongly supported by
the electorate, the Act requires each state and local agency,
such as the Biggs School Board, to adopt conflict of interest
codes. The purposes of the codes are to provide reasonable
assurance that all foreseeable potential conflicts of interest
of public officials will be disclosed and prevented.

It is regrettable that the Biggs trustees believe that the dis-
closure of certain limited information concerning financial
interests tends "to demean, and question the integrity of
citizens who as a civic contribution serve as unpaid members of
boards of education." On the contrary, it is more likely that
the willingness of public officials to disclose certain financial
interests and to disqualify themselves from participating in the
making of decisions that could affect those interests would enhance
their reputations and public confidence in the integrity of govern-
ment.

The Political Reform Act's disclosure requirements are not as
onerous as the Biggs trustees apparently imagine. To help
relieve their concerns, the trustees or their representatives
would be well advised to contact Delbert Spurlock, chief of the
Conflict of Interest Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission, or a member of his staff for legal guidance.

If I can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact
me.

Sincerely,

JOHN KEPLINGER, Chief
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

cc: Governor Edmund G. Brown Jr.
Senator Randolph Collier
Assemblyman Eugene Chappie
Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
Butte County Board of Supervisors
Butte County Counsel
California School Boards Association
Butte County Board of Education
Delbert Spurlock

Biggs Unified School District

BIGGS ELEMENTARY · RICHVALE ELEMENTARY · BIGGS HIGH
POST OFFICE BOX 397
BIGGS, CALIFORNIA 95917
(916) 868-1001

DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT                                April 13, 1976                    BOARD OF TRUSTEES
William J. Caven                                                                        CHESTER CARLSON
                                                                                        _______________
                                                                                        _______________
                                                                                        MERRILL ORME
                                                                                        Tom Vanderfork and
                                                                                        Provisional Appointee
                                                                                        Dr. Craig Morrison

Honorable Edmund G. Brown, Jr., Governor
State of California
State Capitol Building
Capitol Mall
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Governor Brown:

     At its regular meeting on April 5, 1976 the Biggs Unified School District Board
of Trustees approved the enclosed resolution and directed that a copy to sent to you.

                                                                    Sincerely,

                                                                    William J. Caven
                                                                    Acting Superintendent

jms

Enclosures

cc  Senator Randolph Collier
    Assemblyman Eugene Chappie
    Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
    Butte County Board of Supervisors
    Butte County Counsel
    California School Boards Association
    Butte County Board of Education
    Boards of Education  Bangor Union School District
                        Butte Community College
                        Chico Unified School District
                        Durham Unified School District
                        Feather Falls Union School District
                        Golden Feather Union School District
                        Gridley Union Elementary School District
                        Gridley Union High School District
                        Manzanita School District
                        Oroville Elementary School District
                        Oroville Union High School District
                        Palermo Union School District
                        Paradise Unified School District
                        Pioneer Union School District
                        Thermalito Union School District

RESOLUTION TO EXEMPT BOARDS OF EDUCATION OF CALIFORNIA FROM
THE PROVISIONS OF PROPOSITION NINE, RELATING TO THE REQUIREMENT
FOR FILING ECONOMIC INTEREST STATEMENTS

WHEREAS the State of California has enacted legislation generally referred to as
the "Conflict of Interest Code" (Government Code 87300 et. seq.) requiring all
board of Education members of the State to supply certain information relative
to their personal income and possessions, (such information to become a matter
of public record); and

WHEREAS the information required to be supplied by Board of Education members will
have only tenuous and infrequent relation to the expenditure of public school
funds by school districts; and

WHEREAS the public interest would be better served by the filing of sworn statements
by individual board members who may have a potential conflict of interest in a
specific fiscal matter; and

WHEREAS the requirements of Proposition Nine tend to demean, and question the
integrity of, citizens who as a civic contribution serve as unpaid members of
Boards of Education, thus tending to discourage citizens from offering them-
selves for such service; THEREFORE BE IT

RESOLVED, That the Biggs School District Board of Education go on record as
requesting the State Legislature and the Governor of the State of California to
exempt by legislation members of Boards of Education from the provisions of the
above-referred-to legislation; and BE IT

RESOLVED FURTHER, That copies of this Resolution be sent to the Butte County
Board of Supervisors, Butte County Counsel, Senator Randolph Collier, Assembly-
man Eugene Chappie, Assemblyman Pauline Davis, California School Boards
Association, Butte County Board of Education, and all district Boards of Educa-
tion of Butte County.

I, William J. Caven, Secretary of the Board of Education of the Biggs Unified
School District, Butte County, State of California, do hereby certify the fore-
going to be a true and correct copy of a resolution adopted by the Governing
Body of the aforesaid school district at a regular meeting held on the fifth
(5th) day of April, 1976, as the same appears on record in my office.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I have hereunto set my hand this sixth (6th) day of
April, 1976.

William J. Caven
Secretary, Board of Trustees
Biggs Unified School District

jms

sections.question: Does the exemption legislation sought by the Biggs Board of Trustees further the purposes of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: The answer is that it does not.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether proposed legislation to exempt school board members from the Political Reform Act's disclosure provisions would further the purposes of the Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that exemption legislation does not further the purposes of the Political Reform Act, as disclosure of financial interests by public officials enhances rather than diminishes public confidence in government integrity.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (April 13, 1976 letter from Biggs Board to Governor Brown) and FPPC response (May 25, 1976 letter from John Keplinger). Extracted information focuses on the FPPC response letter which contains clear question and conclusion sections. Minor OCR artifacts noted but did not impede understanding.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the exemption legislation sought by the Biggs Board of Trustees further the purposes of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The answer is that it does not.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that the Biggs Unified School District's proposed legislation to exempt school board members from Political Reform Act disclosure requirements does not further the Act's purposes, which were endorsed by 70% of voters in 1974.
